data.title: MARO: Elevated Risk Due To Bitcoin Exposure
data.description:
Overview

YieldMax funds have earned a reputation for providing investors with a dividend yield that can be life changing under the right circumstances. I've covered a ton of these funds in the past, and I've always avoided a few specific ETFs due to the speculative nature of the underlying business. YieldMax MARA Option Income Strategy ETF (MARO) is one of the ETFs that I avoided because of the level of uncertainty around the business and the large levels of volatility. However, it's this level of volatility that allows MARO to reward shareholders with a distribution rate that currently sits around 125%!

MARO implements an option strategy that aims to harness the volatility of MARA Holdings (MARA). If you aren't familiar, MARA is one of the leading Bitcoin (BTC-USD) mining and digital asset technology companies in the world. The core of their business is essentially operating Bitcoin mining machines as well as validating transactions and securing the blockchain. Additionally, the business holds a large Bitcoin treasury, which causes the share price of the stock to change rapidly. If we look at the price of Bitcoin and MARA below, we can see the clear correlation over the last few years.

As we all know, Bitcoin is one of the most volatile assets on the planet, and this has always caused me to stay away from MARO. YieldMax funds have a poor reputation for deteriorating investor capital, and I feared that a decline in Bitcoin would cause MARO's price to collapse. While MARO is trading substantially below its inception price, the decline isn't nearly as bad as some YieldMax peers. MARO launched recently during December 2024, and the ETF has a gross expense ratio of 0.99%. Looking at the performance since inception, we can see how the price has declined by over 49%. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits around a loss of 13%. So let's look at the fund strategy to determine whether or not MARO is worth the risk.

Fund Strategy

MARO is a synthetic option ETF that is aimed at capturing the volatility of MARA. The term 'synthetic' means that MARO doesn't actually own any common shares of MARA, so it's essentially writing options on a stock it doesn't own. The synthetic nature of the fund means that there is an increased risk profile since MARO can experience price losses that are much larger than the actual declines that Mara Holdings.

Over time, the price disconnect can increase substantially, especially if Bitcoin experiences a large run upward. So if you want exposure to Bitcoin or Mara Holdings, you'd be better off simply buying the underlying of each. For instance, MARO's holdings reveal that it doesn't actually hold any common shares or Bitcoin.

MARO is best utilized by income investors that want to collect a high stream of cash flow. Investors should be aware that following the fund's price decline so far, the ETF is very unlikely to experience any sort of positive growth, price resilience, or stability in the future. The highly volatile nature of Bitcoin and MARA makes me certain that MARO isn't really useful for anything else except income generation.

In order to generate the necessary income to fuel distributions, the fund chooses to simultaneously purchase ATM (at-the-money) call options and sell put options. The prospectus explains that in order to generate the option premiums, MARO sells OTM (out-of-the-money) call options that typically have a strike price somewhere between 0% and 15% above the current market price of MARA. The fund documents provided the helpful table below that can help explain the process.

However, a significant drawback of option funds is the capped upside potential. Growth is essentially limited to whatever the selected strike price is. So if MARO writes an option and selects a strike price that is 5% above MARA's market price, but MARA increases by 15%, MARO's growth is capped at that 5% premium strike price. When this strategy is implemented over a prolonged bull market, MARO is aligned to underperform. The best-case scenario would be for MARA and Bitcoin to trade sideways or slowly rise for an extended period of time, so that MARO can capture both the growth while also rewarding shareholders with the premiums.

Performance Scenarios And Risk

Since MARO's inception is so recent, we are working with a limited performance history to reference. When including all distributions that were paid out, MARO's total return has closely tracked the overall performance of MARA Holdings. As we can see below, MARA has a total loss of about 12.4%, while MARO's total return sits at a loss slightly over 13%. There are a few scenarios that can play out here.

Scenarios

  1. MARA Holdings Rises In Value: In this scenario, MARO would likely underperform since the upside participation is capped by the option strategy. This is especially true if MARA's price appreciates rapidly over a short period of time. Additionally, as MARO's price increases, the distributions may also increase. This would be the most ideal scenario for the fund.
  2. Bitcoin stabilizes and MARA Trades Sideways: If prices were to remain flat for MARA, MARO would likely see lower payouts, but they would become more consistent in amount. Due to the generous payouts, there's a possibility that MARO's price would ultimately trend downward if the payouts were not reduced from their current levels. However, a sideways moving price would still provide an environment where the total return can be positive as the distributions are paid out every four weeks.
  3. MARA's Price Decline Continues: Bitcoin has crossed new highs recently, and if the price retracts from here, MARA is likely to also suffer from a downside move. In this scenario, I would expect higher levels of volatility and for MARO's distributions to remain in their elevated range. However, the total return of MARO is likely to remain negative, depending on how drastic the downside movement is.

These are all theoretical possibilities for MARO, and I've used the performance of other YieldMax funds as a reference. MARO isn't the only fund that has some sort of indirect exposure to the crypto markets, so there are plenty of examples to choose from. Some of YieldMax's relevant funds with indirect crypto exposure are the following:

  • Yieldmax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY): Writes options on Strategy (MSTR), which also has a ton of Bitcoin exposure.
  • YieldMax COIN Option Income Strategy ETF (CONY): Writes options on Coinbase (COIN).
  • YieldMax Bitcoin Option Income Strategy ETF (YBIT): Aims to directly seek exposure to the price of Bitcoin.
  • YieldMax Crypto Industry & Tech Portfolio Option Income ETF (LFGY): Tracks a portfolio of crypto industry and technology companies.

Looking at the price history of these peers below, we can see a very similar story play out on a YTD basis. The history below is limited due to the fact that LFGY was recently launched in January of 2025. Despite Bitcoin being up YTD, all of the mentioned funds below have seen their price decline. These comparisons are just to demonstrate that this is the inherent risk involved with synthetic option ETFs. The price is very likely to see continued downside, even if the underlying assets they track appreciate in value over time.

This is why I want to put emphasis on the fact that MARO should only be utilized by investors that specifically seek income. A fund like MARO may not be a simple buy and hold position, especially due to MARA's speculative nature. Unlike businesses like Coinbase, MARA Holdings doesn't have the most reassuring earnings history. MARA has sporadic revenues and operating expenses that can be difficult to predict every quarter. While there may be short-term catalysts for the company, MARA isn't a stock that I personally feel confident in holding for an extended period. In comparison, a company like Coinbase has different revenue streams that help create a healthier looking balance sheet.

Despite all of the optimism in the crypto markets this year, the Bitcoin cycle can shift. If there is a large retraction in the price of Bitcoin, I anticipate that MARO will experience a sizeable decline. As the price declines, so will the distributions, which makes it harder to turn a profit on this fund. So while I am fairly optimistic on the future of crypto, I would prefer to initiate a position in MARO when the cycle isn't at the height of its optimism.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared distribution of $2.3718 per share, the estimated annual yield currently sits around a whopping 125%. When we talk about a dividend yield this high, the ultimate end goal would be to reach 'house money' status. This is the cross point where you receive 100% of your initial investment back in the form of distributions. Since MARO offers a yield that is over 100%, an investor should technically be able to collect their initial investment back in less than twelve months, assuming that payouts remain the same.

The only issue is that payouts tend to be wildly different every four weeks. A new payout is declared every four weeks and is determined by the underlying success of the option strategy, the price movement of MARA, and overall market volatility. As we can see below, the payouts have been varied since the fund's inception. Only four weeks ago, MARO experienced the lowest payout since the fund's inception, so conditions around the yield can change rapidly.

If you haven't already, I would highly suggest going to the YieldMax site and reaching out to them to request being added to the distribution list. You will receive emails that detail the specific payouts each week. As we can see below, the latest distribution brings the estimated annual rate to slightly over 125% and was paid out on 7/18/2025. What stands out the most to me is that 0% of the distribution was funded using the return of capital.

Usually, YieldMax funds put an emphasis on return of capital because they reduce the overall tax burden that investors have to deal with. For instance, I previously covered YieldMax MSFT Option Income Strategy ETF (MSFO) and over 92% of the distribution was paid using return of capital. Distributions are likely classified as net investment income, which is considered to be ordinary dividend income. Therefore, we can assume that the majority of the distributions received from MARO will not be tax-efficient, and a position may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate MARO as a hold for now due to the speculative nature of the underlying focus on MARA Holdings. The fund will also be influenced by the movement of Bitcoin, and I am concerned about how MARO will perform if the crypto sentiment shifts. If Bitcoin and MARA experience a sizeable retraction, MARO will continue to deteriorate investor capital over time. Additionally, the distributions aren't tax-efficient, which means investors may need to consider the possibility of a larger tax burden at the end of the year.

data.title: PCB Bancorp: Downgrading To Hold; Earnings Outlook Remains Rosy
data.description:
Since I last adopted a buy rating on PCB Bancorp (PCB) in January, the stock price has grown by over 14%. Further, the company has reported its earnings for 4Q’24 and 1Q’25, both of which were in line with my expectations. To refresh my investment thesis, I’m going to start by looking at the issue of the wildfires, which was one of the most pressing matters at the time of my last report.

Loan Losses to Normalize Following the Wildfires

According to the earnings presentation, around 62% of PCB Bancorp’s Commercial Real Estate (“CRE”) and 44% of Commercial & Industrial (“C&I”) loans were based in Los Angeles County, California, at the end of March 2025. As a result, the portfolio was exposed to devastation from the wildfires in the region earlier this year.

As per the details given in the earnings release, non-performing loans rose to 0.23% of total loans by the end of March 2025 from 0.18% of total loans at the end of December 2024.

Mar'25 Dec'24 Mar'24
NPLs to Loans HFI 0.23% 0.18% 0.21%
NPAs to Total Assets 0.20% 0.15% 0.17%
Classified Assets to Total Assets 0.26% 0.23% 0.27%

Source: 1Q'25 Earnings Release

Although the credit quality of the loan book worsened in line with my expectations, PCB Bancorp did not raise its provisions for loan losses by as much as I had anticipated. In my last report, I projected the provision expense to jump up to $2.0 million in the first quarter of the year before normalizing from the second quarter onwards. In reality, the provision expense for 1Q’25 was reported at $1.6 million only.

From the criticized assets figure, it’s evident that the wildfires have not had much of an impact on PCB Bancorp’s profitability despite the company’s exposure to Los Angeles. Therefore, I’m assuming the provision expense would’ve returned to a normal level of around $1.0 million a quarter from 2Q’25 onwards.

I also believe that PCB’s riskiness has dipped significantly since my last report because the wildfires did not turn out to be too devastating for the company.

Fall in Deposit Costs Likely to Outweigh a Fall in Loan Yields

PCB Bancorp’s net interest margin has been volatile and directionless lately, rising in one quarter and falling in the next. Going forward, the biggest factor that could affect the margin is the Fed funds rate trajectory. Following the 100-basis-point cut last year, I’m expecting the Fed to cut rates by 50 basis points later this year. The futures market is also mostly expecting something similar, according to CME’s FedWatch Tool. Going forward, the Fed funds rate cuts will have a mixed impact on PCB Bancorp’s net interest margin.

On the one hand, the rate cuts could quickly reduce the average loan yield. As per details given in the presentation, around 51% of loans held-for-investment will re-price within 12 months. This is mainly attributable to a large balance of variable-rate loans, which made up 44% of total loans at the end of March 2025.

On the other hand, the rate cuts could also reduce the deposit costs. Savings, NOW, and money-market deposits altogether made up 19% of total deposits at the end of March 2025. These deposits can be re-priced soon after every rate change. Additionally, time deposits totaling $1,633 million will mature within 12 months, representing 60% of total deposits, as per details given in the 10-Q filing. Hence, around 79% of the deposit book can be re-priced downward within a year of a rate cut.

Considering these factors, I’m assuming the net interest margin remained rangebound in the second quarter. Further, I’m expecting the margin to grow by 6 basis points in the second half of 2025.

Slight Deceleration Likely for Loan Growth

PCB Bancorp’s loan growth remained quite strong in 4Q’24 and 1Q’25, in line with the impressive growth seen over the last few years. I think loan growth could slow down a bit for the remainder of the year because of weaknesses in the market. A majority of PCB Bancorp’s operations are based in California, with a concentration in Los Angeles County. Other regions are New York, New Jersey, Texas, Georgia, and Washington.

As seen below, Los Angeles’ unemployment rate has been quite high lately, which means the business activity is not as healthy as in 2023. This bodes ill for loan demand.

Other labor markets are also not doing that well. Although initial jobless claims have declined in the country for the fifth straight week, continuing claims are at the highest level since November 2021, according to news reports. This means that people who have previously been laid off are finding it hard to find jobs, but new layoffs are being kept at bay for now.

As the Federal Reserve and World Bank are projecting the USA’s GDP growth to halve to 1.4% this year from 2.8% last year, I think business activity, and consequently loan demand, will likely take a hit in the remainder of 2025.

Overall, I’m expecting the loan book to have grown by 2% in 2Q’25. Further, I’m expecting it to continue to grow by 2% every quarter through the end of 2025. For the full year, I’m expecting a loan growth of 10%, which is quite high but below the historical average. The 5-year compounded annual growth rate till 2024 was 12.6%.

Now Expecting an EPS of $2.13 for 2025

PCB Bancorp’s earnings for both 4Q’24 and 1Q’25 were mostly in line with my expectations given in my January report. Going forward, I’m expecting the high, albeit below-average, loan growth to drive earnings. Further, the slight net interest margin growth will help support the bottom line.

On the other hand, the high provision expense reported in 1Q’25 will drag earnings this year, even though I’m expecting it to normalize from 2Q’25 onwards.

To arrive at my EPS estimate, I’m assuming the non-interest income will remain steady at the level seen over the last couple of years. Further, I’m assuming the efficiency ratio will remain near 55%, which is the average for the last three reported quarters.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report an EPS of $2.13 for 2025, up by 23% year-over-year. Compared to my last report, wherein I gave an EPS estimate of $2.06, I haven’t changed my estimate much.

Downgrading to Hold

I adopted a buy rating on PCB Bancorp with a December 2025 target price of $20.5 when I last wrote about the company in January of this year. Since then, the stock price has increased by over 14%. To update my investment thesis, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples given in the chart below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $19.4 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $24.6 for the end of 2025.

The average December 2025 price target comes out to $22.0. This is higher than my previous target price of $20.5, mostly because the P/E and P/TB multiples are higher now as I take simple moving averages.

My new target price suggests a small price upside of 2.0% from the July 18 closing market price of $21.55. Adding the company’s dividend yield of 3.7% to the price upside gives a total expected return of 5.8%. Although I’ve raised my target price, the total expected return is smaller now compared to when I last wrote about PCB because the stock’s market price has rallied since that report. Based on the updated total expected return, I’m downgrading PCB Bancorp to a hold rating from my previous rating of buy.

data.title: MGP Ingredients: Navigating A Sharp Whiskey Production Slump
data.description:
MGP Ingredients, Inc. (MGPI) is set to report the company’s Q2 results on the 31st of July. The spirit ingredient and branded spirit producer is seeing the effects of weakening demand for alcohol - elevated whiskey inventories have continued to cause havoc on demand for brown goods, dragging MGP’s outlook. Visibility for an earnings trend turnaround remains low.

I cautiously remained at a Hold rating in my previous January article on the stock, titled “MGP Ingredients: Prolonged Spirit Industry Weakness Drags Outlook”. Since then, MGP’s stock has lost a further -17% of its value, underperforming S&P 500’s gain of 5%.

Q2 Preview: MGP’s Weakness Will Continue as Whiskey Production Slumps

MGP’s upcoming Q2 report is likely to be weak. Wall Street expects revenues to come in at just $138.6 million, down by -27.4% year-on-year. Alongside significantly lower revenues, the adjusted EPS is expected to plummet to just $0.66, down by -61.5% year-on-year through significant margin deterioration.

The weak expectations for Q2 have clear merits in MGP’s continued financial performance. The upcoming report follows a Q1 where MGP’s revenues declined by -28.7% and the adjusted EPS declined by -66.4%, and where MGP reaffirmed a 2025 revenue guidance range of $520-540 million. The range suggests a mid-point revenue decline of -24.7%, not suggesting a significant rebound in upcoming quarters. Wall Street’s second quarter expectation only suggests a moderate sequential revenue improvement from an especially weak first quarter.

MGP's distilling solutions segment is still the major cause for concern - MGP has continued to see the effects of heightened industry-wide whiskey inventories, weakening the outlook for brown goods sales as whiskey production is declining sharply. Weaker demand is also causing significant pricing pressure in the segment, and distilling solutions is ultimately expected by MGP’s management to halve in revenues during 2025, causing nearly all of the guided revenue decline for MGP. Industry-wide whiskey production data clearly supports the narrative – while reported at a significant delay, TTB data currently shows a massively accelerated production slump, reaching -25.2% year-on-year for whiskey for the January-February period. For comparison, whiskey production only declined -4.5% in 2024, already causing a significant decline in demand for MGP’s brown goods. MGP’s management still saw difficulty in predicting the production reset’s timing and extent in the May Q1 earnings call, which in my opinion signals a weak short-term demand outlook. As such, Wall Street’s expectation of a weak Q2 is warranted, and the outlook remains uncertain past the quarter.

Ultimately, the longer-term view depends on consumer demand for whiskey rather than temporary production levels. The currently depressed level of production doesn’t reflect quite stable long-term whiskey volume expectations; while per-capita consumption of whiskey is expected to decline in the U.S. due to younger people drinking less, total consumption should stay relatively stable over the long term. With a currently very cloudy industry outlook, the Q2 report’s focus point is, in my opinion, mostly in the management’s comments on the forward outlook. The second quarter will more than likely show very weak earnings, but the long-term outlook should be anchored into normalized whiskey production levels.

Premium Spirits Could Create Growth

With the distilling solutions segment’s decline, the focus in MGP has shifted towards the company’s own branded spirit portfolio. Meanwhile, the total segment showed a -3.8% revenue decline in Q1 to $48.2 million, branded spirits also have positives to note—the value and mid-price range showed a decline alongside weak industry demand in a turbulent consumer environment, but MGP’s premium plus portfolio even showed 6.8% growth to $22.3 million. Growth in higher-priced products drove up the segment’s gross margin mix. The Penelope and Rebel 100 bourbons and El Mayor tequila have shown good sales gains on the retail level as MGP is focusing on fewer brands with product innovation and distribution expansion, looking to drive up the branded spirits segment.

Ultimately, the premium plus portfolio of MGP’s branded spirits is still a small part of the business, and the distilling solutions segment remains a critical piece – potential growth in branded spirits is a moderate positive, while MGP’s critical distilling solutions segment will likely continue to struggle.

Updated Valuation: MGP Remains an Uncertain Investment

I updated my discounted cash flow [DCF] model for MGP’s stock for a fair value estimate. I lowered my revenue growth expectations, still assuming a rebound through the 2027-2029 period after a very sharp 2024-2025 decline and slight further issues in 2026. I now assume MGP’s growth to stabilize at 2% instead of 2.5% previously, driven by very slow growth in alcohol consumption but MGP’s better potential in premium spirits.

Given the lower expected level in revenues, I now assume the EBIT margin to ultimately stabilize at 20% instead of 22% previously. The lower growth does enable MGP to invest less in inventory put-away and capital expenditures as the company targets just $36 million in 2025 CapEx, improving the cash flow conversion outlook for upcoming years.

The updated estimates put MGP’s fair value at $35.56, 19% above the stock price at the time of writing – the stock still has some estimated upside. Again, though, MGP’s outlook is very cloudy as well, and the estimated upside merely represents a modest margin of safety. For now, continued uncertainty in the industry still warrants caution in MGP as an investment. The lower price could represent a buying opportunity, but great returns would require a very good industry rebound.

The fair value estimate is down from $48.32 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.90%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a low 2.49% interest rate, annualized from MGP’s Q1 interest expenses and debt. I still expect a fair 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.44% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. Seeking Alpha now estimates MGP’s beta at 0.75. With an ESG addon of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 9.71% and the WACC at 7.90%.

Takeaway

MGP’s upcoming Q2 report will likely reflect a very sharp weakness in the distilling solutions segment as industry-wide whiskey production has plummeted in recent months. Weaker pricing power and negative operating leverage are likely to pile on top, creating very weak earnings prospects as the industry navigates unhealthy whiskey inventories. Over the long term, industry conditions should recover, but recovery timing remains uncertain. Growth in premium branded spirits could offset some of the distilling solution decline. With a current valuation that has very uncertain upside potential, I remain with a hold rating for MGP Ingredients.

data.title: JNK Can Benefit From Lower Rates; Cuts May Be Pushed Out Due To Higher Inflation
data.description:
The SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) is an index-tracking bond strategy designed to provide investors with diversified exposure to high-yield bonds.

JNK is a well-diversified strategy with 1,208 individual issuances within the portfolio strategy. The largest holding in the portfolio strategy, excluding cash, has a weight of 0.54%, suggesting limited concentration risk across the strategy.

The average option-adjusted duration of the portfolio is 4.08 years, providing a certain level of interest rate risk that may benefit the strategy during periods of decreasing rates. The average yield to maturity across the portfolio is 8.42%, well above the index of 7.32%, providing investors with substantially higher income potential by comparison.

High-yield bonds generally trade similarly to stocks, in which market risk and economic stress can impact each respective security class. The theory behind this is that high-yield issuers’ operations can be more dependent on economic cycles and rates, placing increased risk on the enterprises when compared to investment-grade issuers. During periods of economic stress, particularly when considering the current tariff environment, sales may reflect slower growth or declines, resulting in substantial pressure on margins and the organizations’ ability to cover interest payments and maturities. The rate market can also hold substantial weight for high-yield issuers, as higher-rate markets may lead to higher costs of debt issuances, potentially resulting in margin strain.

In terms of rates, bond pricing tends to have an inverse relationship to interest rates, resulting in issuance price appreciation (decline) during periods of declining (increasing) interest rates. With Wall Street consensus expecting 75-100bps in rate cuts by the end of 2025, JNK may exhibit positive returns with the presumption of the Federal Reserve cutting rates. There may be two sides to the coin, as the recent inflation data may push out any hopes for a rate cut by the Fed, potentially resulting in higher-for-longer interest rates.

JNK is diversified across industries, with the top sector weightings being consumer cyclicals at 23.11%, communications at 18.37%, and energy at 16.82%.

In general, consumer cyclicals may face certain headwinds resulting from the current tariff policies imposed by the US. The sector may be impacted by both increased costs in the supply chain and lower consumer spending as a result of tighter discretionary budgets and inflationary pressures. Consumer sentiment has been teetering, weighed by inflation following the pandemic as well as new fears of higher costs resulting from trade tariffs. The recent July report by the University of Michigan suggests that consumer sentiment is improving off of April/May 2025 lows with long-term inflation expectations coming down.

This may take a turn in next month’s report following the return to inflationary pressures, likely the result of import duties. June 2025 inflation came in at 2.7%, up from 2.4% in May 2025. Depending on consumer spending habits reflective of the current economic data, consumer cyclicals may face some pressure in the coming months as a result of the return of increasing inflationary pressures.

Communications may be a mixed bag in terms of growth and stability. In general, the consumer transition from traditional pay-TV to streaming continues to progress in favor of streaming services. It is estimated that 5% of pay-TV households will transition out of these services in 2025, potentially resulting in an increased dependency on Internet streaming services. In turn, this transition can benefit bandwidth/wireless providers, potentially leading consumers to increase Internet connectivity in order to support streaming services, benefiting telco services companies that focus on fiber and Internet connectivity.

Energy may also be relatively mixed, but supportive, as oil prices face downside pressure while natural gas improves on pricing. In general, O&G producers have improved their respective capital structures since the pandemic, resulting in a lower debt-to-equity load and improving solvency & sustainability. One of the key themes across the industry is disciplined production, meaning that the flywheel of capital spending to drive growth is no longer a leading factor for organizations. Disciplined spending, paying down debt, and returning cash to shareholders are the primary initiatives across the industry, allowing for more stability during the O&G pricing cycles.

One of JNK’s larger company concentrations is in Venture Global (VG) at nearly 2% of the total portfolio weight. Venture Global is one of the leading LNG producers & exporters in the US, recently bringing its equity to the public markets at the start of 2025. The organization has relatively stable cash flows through the use of long-term, 20+ year sales/purchase agreements for LNG. In general, midstream operators will raise capital through the debt markets to construct facilities. These large, fixed assets provide bondholders with some certainty, especially when cash flows are relatively known through long-term agreements.

ETF Details

Comparing portfolio strategies, JNK’s fees are relatively on par with peer high-yield strategies at 40bps. From a total return perspective, JNK has underperformed its peers on a 1-year and 3-year timeframe, suggesting that JNK may not be as optimal a strategy as peer portfolios.

Though the VanEck International High-Yield Bond ETF (IHY) has outperformed on a total return basis, the portfolio is relatively smaller with only $41mm in assets under management. This compares to JNK with over $8b in AUM.

In terms of liquidity, an average of 4mm shares trade hands daily, providing investors with enough depth to actively manage a position in JNK with minimal spread risk.

Additional Risks To JNK

JNK inherently invests in risky issuances when compared to investment-grade strategies. Investments are relatively well distributed across investment grades, with the majority of holdings residing in Ba3 at 31.52%. Roughly 14% of the fund’s investments are in C-rated and below issuances, potentially adding certain exposure to increased economic stress resulting from higher import duty costs.

High-yield issuances should not be seen as stable income security, as these issuances may exhibit substantially higher pricing volatility when compared to investment-grade issuances. High-yield issuers may also exhibit more operational risk when compared to investment-grade issuers, adding an additional layer of risk for investors. Given the diversification of JNK, this may not be as relevant of an issue when compared to those investing in individual issuances.

Final Thoughts

JNK may be an appealing investment opportunity during a period of interest rate decline. Given the current inflationary environment and potential higher-for-longer interest rate policy, I believe it may be prudent to hold off on investing in JNK. I do believe there will be a more opportune time to invest in this ETF as economic conditions improve. I am recommending JNK with a HOLD rating.

data.title: Faraday Future: Liquidity In Reverse Even As Stock Surges
data.description:
Future Intelligent Electric (FFAI) is down roughly 55% since I last covered the ticker at the end of 2024 with a sell rating on concerns that consistent quarters of net losses and negative free cash flow were pushing liquidity towards a point of no return. The central thesis in that article was that the company would increasingly be unable to fund its liquidity gap, raising going concern risk and pushing the shares to trade lower than NASDAQ's $1 minimum price listing requirement. FFAI is up 31% over the last six months, currently trading hands for nearly $2 per share and pushing above its 52-week low of $0.83 per share. The rally represents a mea culpa for the bears, albeit one that has reversed in recent days. There is currently a 25% short interest in the EV upstart. The surge has been driven by a mix of enthusiasm over FFAI's fundraising and its efforts to brand itself as an artificial intelligence play in a market where enthusiasm for these AI tickers continues to ramp up.

The California-based EV upstart reported a fiscal 2025 first-quarter net loss of $10.28 million, its lowest net loss figure in over 3 years, with negative free cash flow maintained at a pace broadly unchanged from its prior quarters. This was $21.86 million during the first quarter, with the trailing 12-month figure at $84.8 million. FFAI's total cash and cash equivalents position ended the first quarter at $9.5 million with $68.5 million of total debt. Deliveries remain inconsequential, as just two FF 91 2.0 were delivered during the first quarter for a total revenue of $300,000. Hence, FFAI's financials continue to congeal around a bearish case that places break-even beyond the horizon of its current operational gearing, one where negative operating cash flows continually exceed liquidity to bring going concern risk into the rearview mirror. I remain extremely bearish on the company's outlook, with the shares rated a strong sell. The rally, in my opinion, will be transient, and the common shares will resume a decline on future earnings reports that show a continuation of net losses and cash burn.

Liquidity In Reverse And The Risk To Bears

How's FFAI able to continue operations even after consistent quarters of negative free cash flow? It's common shares. The company has been aggressively selling shares. Its average diluted shares outstanding ended the first quarter at 75.7 million, up 392% over its year-ago period. The current rally provides management with a material salvo for being able to sell more shares to continue to expand a liquidity balance that would otherwise be unable to meet FFAI's quarterly cash burn rate as of the last figure for the first quarter. Hence, bears are in a tricky position as FFAI has continually been able to starve off the worst-case scenario of its bearish case.

Fundraising efforts have also been ramping up, with FFAI recently announcing that it had secured roughly $105 million in new cash financing. The funding will mainly come in the form of unsecured convertible notes and warrants to acquire additional FFAI common stock. The investors are an aggregate of Middle Eastern institutional investors, a region the company is targeting to sell its $249,000 FF 91 2.0. FFAI has delivered just 16 cars since deliveries began in 2023. The company delivered 10 vehicles in 2023, but this dropped to 4 vehicles in 2024, with two vehicles delivered in the first quarter of this year. FFAI also possibly faces SEC enforcement action following the results of a three-year fraud investigation.

The Wells Notice, highlighted by FFAI when it announced its new financing round, is centered on statements related to the company's 2021 merger with the blank check company Property Solutions Acquisition Corp. FFAI remains a sell on a vehicle that, in my opinion, is priced far too high to make an impact on an increasingly crowded global market for electric vehicles. The company also faces a Sisyphean task ahead of ramping revenue with a cost base and negative free cash flow position that is far too high and consistently negative. The recent fundraise represents a significant win for FFAI, but downstream dilution will be material, and the outlook for the common shares remains, in my opinion, quite weak. This continued dilution of shareholders is a core risk for FFAI bulls, especially if operating cash burn continues to come in hot every quarter. Against its trailing 12-month free cash flow and assuming no further cash is raised, the financing raised should extend the company's run rate into 2026. FFAI has proven adept at raising new funds, hence, I do not see the worst-case scenario for its liquidity gap playing out in 2025.

data.title: Canadian Natural Will See Its Already Impressive Margins Boosted By LNG Canada
data.description:
Introduction

Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) (TSX:CNQ:CA) stands as a bastion of Canadian Energy, being one of the largest independent O&G producers in the country. It currently has a vast set of assets, mainly in Western Canada, and has become a key player in the energy landscape.

With the development of LNG Canada and the sound economics of the legacy oil sands business (even if I admit that I'm not the biggest expert on Canadian oil sands), I believe that Canadian Natural Resources is poised to gain an advantage of the Canadian energy landscape

Operation Overview

CNQ has diversified into different O&G sources, with its E&P North America being at the core of its asset portfolio. The segment is mainly based in the Western Canadian sedimentary basin. This has allowed CNQ to become the 2nd largest Canadian natural gas producer with strong infrastructure in areas like Montney and Deep Basin that are producing at fairly low costs, with overall production reaching 2.45 Bcf/d in Q1. In total, natural gas accounts for 27% of the production mix.

Looking at crude, CNQ is the largest crude oil producer in Canada, with around 65% of its output being high-value SCO, light crude oil, and NGLs, and is currently expecting to produce between 1,106 and 1,142 Mbbl/d through its vast resource base in North America, the North Sea, and offshore Africa. Looking at Africa, CNQ currently has some strong assets in Ivory Coast and South Africa, though they only contribute a small portion of the overall revenue for the company. The same can be said for the CNQ’s North Sea operations with relatively few assets, as the company is currently decommissioning them, as future development might no longer be economical. In all, North America Revenue reached $13.39B in the LTM, while the North Sea and Offshore Africa contributed around $700MM combined in the same timespan.

And for the specific oil sands, CNQ has some of the best mining assets (oil sands extraction resembles more to mining than classical O&G extraction). This provides a source of long-life and low-decreasing synthetic crude oil with major assets including the Horizon Oil Sands and the Athabasca Oil Sands Project. In Q1, the segment produced around 595,000 bpd of SCO, which generated $12.87B in sales in the LTM.

Current Dynamics

CNQ had a strong start of the year thanks to the debottlenecking of projects in its Oil Sands segment, which allowed the company to reach a record-high utilization rate. It also posted better results from its recently acquired Duvernay assets. This has allowed CNQ to reduce its CapEx plans by around $100MM.

On a more macro basis, CNQ has been influenced by the current volatility in the global oil market on the back of the current tensions in the Middle East. And while CNQ has posted strong prices on its SCO, WTI and natural gas have been more than volatile and going back to multi-year lows while the Western Canadian AECO benchmark remains weak. Though the ramping up of LNG Canada will certainly increase demand, supporting a higher price near term.

Valuation

Canadian Natural has an inline current valuation. If we compare it to other peers, the company's P/E is in line with the broad sector at 10.98, while its EV/EBITDA stands at 6.22. Current FCF yield is strong at 10.3%, which highlights how profitable the company is on the back of the highly efficient oil sands segment

Growth has been muted over the last 3 years due to the overall decrease in commodity prices. Though I expect CNQ to come back to incremental growth on the back of several factors, such as its improvement in efficiencies in its Oil Sands department plus expansions like the Pike and Jackfish projects. I also believe that the LNG Canada facility will be a tremendous opportunity for the company. For a little history, until now, natural gas producers in Western Canada faced a massive issue, which was that their serving market was restrained. Nat Gas had only access to an already oversaturated North American distribution network, creating a significant discount on the AECO benchmark compared to Henry Hub. Now with a whole new market to tap, which will create a long-term structural demand and allow CNQ to either supply the domestic market or the LNG facilities, depending on the potential returns.

Looking at profitability, the company shines through, as highlighted by its A+ grade from Seeking Alpha. CNQ has some of the best bottom-line margins in the O&G industry due to the nature of the oil sands business. The operations are not like shale wells that depreciate quickly and require constant capital flows to maintain a good enough operation. Sands offer a more predictable volume of oil through decades, which drastically requires less CapEx to maintain. Though it is important to note that in its full cycle, starting an oil sands mine requires an enormous amount of capital, only viable at a high overall WTI price. However, once they’re built, these mines are cheaper to operate, and a larger portion of sales directly translates into net income. That’s why, despite a smaller size and smaller integration, CNQ is able to provide more cash flow and net income on its revenue base compared to the likes of Exxon and Chevron. I believe that it will increase in the near future, as CNQ gets more pricing power from its sales of natural gas through Canada LNG.

Looking at capital return to shareholders, CNQ has quite an interesting policy targeting stability, which in my mind is a strong selling point compared to the rest of the O&G industry. If net debt falls below CAD$12B, 100% of FCF is distributed back to shareholders. Thus, CNQ has bought back close to 300MM shares since 2020 while posting a current dividend yield of 5.23%, on the higher end of integrated oil companies. Growth has also been stellar, with a massive 15.63% CAGR growth rate in the company’s payout.

Risks

After all, CNQ is dependent on commodity prices and the inherent volatility that will affect its overall revenue. Though the company’s legacy oil sands are performing well despite volatility, the company’s heavy crude could see its discount to WTI widen due to potential pipeline bottlenecks. And while the prospects of LNG Canada offer some very good long-term opportunities, the natural gas market is even more volatile, which could affect the company’s bottom line.

Now, if the energy transition happens faster than expected, this would erode the company’s revenue while leaving heavily depreciated stranded assets. Furthermore, changes in policies like the carbon tax and clean fuel standards could have increased costs for the company.

Conclusion

I believe that there are too many tailwinds to ignore for Canadian Natural Resources, such as Canada LNG and the company's highly efficient operation, which allows CNQ to have one of the lowest breakeven prices both on its conventional oil production and on Oil Sands (breakeven below $45 a barrel). Plus, the cautious capital return policies allow the company to weather the downturns of the O&G industry. I rate the company as a strong buy.

data.title: Power Solutions International: Solid Margins, Fuel Flexibility, And A Market Still Ignoring The Cash Flow
data.description:
Power Solutions International (PSIX) is a small capitalization industry technology company that has long been outside trader radar. However, in 2025 the situation has fundamentally changed: the company has returned to strong profitability, reduced debt, established itself in fast-growing segments (especially in data centers and energy), and began to create long-term value through innovation from HVO-ready engines to lithium-ion electrification solutions.

Most investors still look at PSIX as a risky player, mainly due to fluctuating share price, which happens due to low float, but the facts show the opposite: the company has become one of the most effective in its segment. With fast-growing margins, stable cash flows. PSIX becomes a valuable "hidden" opportunity for long-term growth.

Financial Health

Power Solutions is currently demonstrating strong financial health. The company's annual revenue in the past quarter was $516.17 million, and net income was $81.25 million, which means excellent profitability - the net profit margin exceeds 15%. This is an impressive result, given that PSIX was balanced at loss a few years ago. EPS is currently $3.53, which indicates a strong and healthy earnings power. EBITDA is $98.09 million and net income is $81.25 million, so we have high operating efficiency.

PSIX significantly improved its balance. The company has $50.02 million cash, which forms a solid security reserve for both short-term needs and strategic growth. The total debt is around $153.74 million, of which only $24 million is long-term and short-term debt around $15.7 million. The bulk of the remaining amounts is associated with the loan and credit lines of the Weich, but even if we take this out of the equation, the net debt is $103.72 million. This means that the debt still makes up a significant part of the balance sheet - net debt to equity is around 182.28%, but it is important to look at that one number: the covered ratio is as high as 9.22, significantly higher than the industry average, meaning that they have enough to cover themselves at least, to help ease the situation if bad times come.

What about liquidity? The current ratio is 1.16 and the quick ratio is 0.62. Although these are not the ideal numbers, they are not making PSIX at risk for short-term solvency. In addition, PSIX generates solid cash flows, Debt/Free Cash Flow is 15.13, which indicates that the company has the opportunity to reduce debt without needing external capital. Long-term debt amounts to only 15.87% of the total capital, which leads to the conclusion that financial levers are managed and controlled.

In general, PSIX's financial health is strong: the company generates stable profits, has enough money, efficiently reduces debt, and supports sustainable business flows. Although the overall financial leverage remains high, it is based on profitability and the high EBITDA, which allows the company to continue to expand and create value for shareholders.

Catalysts

PSIX is currently operating in a rapidly changing market, where several important external and internal factors act as catalysts for the company's growth.

1. Data centers demand boom and decentralized energy

One of the strongest PSIX short-term and long-term growth engine catalysts is the growth of data centers, especially in North America. With the rapid growth of artificial intelligence (AI), cloud services, and digital infrastructure needs, data centers require more power supplies. PSIX supplies industrial engines and power modules (both internal combustion and electrified solutions), which are important for both constant and uninterrupted power supply.

A this new trend of AI allows the PSIX to a growing segment of data centers, which prioritizes high-power, and as little carbon trace solutions as possible. Certified engines of HVO (Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil) are one of the products as to why PSIX becomes an attractive choice for suppliers. This niche is not yet fully absorbed by the big OEM players, and PSIX's ability to quickly apply solutions gives a strategic advantage.

2. Renewable energy and "fuel neutrality" technology

The PSIX product line is becoming increasingly attractive in terms of sustainability. The company already has products certified with HVOs - a biodiesel variant that allows customers to use existing internal combustion engine infrastructure without any modifications, while reducing emissions to 90%. This is an important step in switching to the "fuel neutrality" strategy, we can see that is happening in Europe as well, Rolls-Royce, Cummins for example, are already certifying themselves in HVO. And Europe does have stricter emissions regulation, yet I think it's going to remain a trend and become a global standard.

In addition, PSIX develops lithium-ion electric modules to replace traditional engines in agricultural, logistics, and special-purpose equipment. This hybrid position is the ability to supply both the traditional and electrified solution - making the company attractive to customers looking for technological flexibility. Since most of the smaller OEM players do not have their own electrification lines, PSIX can become an external partner with a "key to hand" integration.

3. Financial restructuring and decline in credit burden

Another important catalyst is the company's active improvement of financial structure, as I mentioned in the financial health section. Decrease in debt from $ 200+ million a few years ago to $153.74 million. Today, along with a significant cash reserve, it allows to expect even more investor confidence, and from shares rising, it is the case. If PSIX continues to reduce net debt (currently $103.72 million) and reaches a lower DEBT/EBITDA rate, this may be the basis for improving ratings even more, lower interest costs, and better funding conditions in the future.

4. Inclusion in indices and increased visibility

In 2025, PSIX was included in several significant indexes: Russell 2000, Russell 3000, Microcap and MSCI US Small Cap. This automatically included the company in many index funds and ETF portfolios. Such changes often lead to increased stock liquidity and potential institutional investors, pension funds start to be used as a diversifying tool. Since PSIX was previously a little-known player, I remember watching it in OTC before it was listed and buying it at the same listing day (for those who don't know, it was listed this year, so fairly new).

Risks

Although PSIX is currently demonstrating impressive profitability and growth, before investing there are some risks associated with this company. The company operates in a cyclical industry, has considerable financial leverage even though they are dealing with it, still it's a risk and strongly depends on several basic segments.

First of all, PSIX activities remain closely linked to a cycle of capital goods, which means that the demand for customers can fluctuate depending on economic conditions. For example, one of the main segments of the company - industrial equipment (forklifts, agricultural machines, and construction equipment) - is very sensitive to changes in interest rates, construction activity, and raw material cycles. Already in 2025, in the first quarter, this segment shrunk what could be a signal of structural or cyclical challenges.

Second, the company's financial leverage is still high. Although the total debt is decreasing, the net debt is currently $103.72 million, and debt-to-equity exceeds 180%. This indicates that the company remains dependent on borrowed capital and that the ability to service debt may be a burden in the event of an interest hike.

Third, PSIX activities are very focused on North America, especially the US market. This means that any political, tax, or regulatory changes - such as emissions or the use of internal combustion engines - can have a significant effect. If the big states, such as California, will begin to ban massive diesel generators or promote only electrical solutions, PSIX will have to reorient what can be expensive.

Fourth, despite the technological advantages, PSIX operates in an extremely competitive environment. Players like Cummins, Caterpillar, or Geneac have more resources and better developed sales channels. If these competitors decide to go more aggressively into the HVO or electrification markets, PSIX could lose some of their niche advantage.

Valuation

PSIX is currently traded at 24.7x P/E and 22.4x P/B, while EV/EBITDA is about 20.8. This shows that the shares are priced higher than most industrial competitors, but this valuation is partially justified by strong profitability, stable net flow, and growing demand. By comparison, other players in the sector, such as BWEN or BW, operate at a loss or have much weaker valuations (BWEN EV/EBITDA - 10.04; P/B - 0.98) while PSIX maintains high margins and financial stability.

I did DCF, and it shows that the fair value should be around $118.60 per share. At the moment, the market price is currently ~$82.70. This means ~43% theoretical potential for price growth. The model is based on conservative 7.9% WACC, 2.5% perpetual growth, and 70% FCF conversion. Even with moderate growth (10% to 2% over the next five years), PSIX generates significant value.

In summary, although PSIX valuation when looking at peers seems high, most likely this stock is going to be traded above its fair value in the future, as it happens most of the time, so there is some upside left.

Summary

Power Solutions International (PSIX) demonstrates impressive financial and operating recovery - profitability high, debt is controlled, and cash flows are stable. The company is actively positioning in growing markets, especially in data centers and decentralized energy sectors, where HVO and electrification solutions give a competitive advantage. The bunch of catalysts - from financial restructuring to inclusion in indexes - maintains strong growth potential. Although cyclical and regulatory risks remain, PSIX has proven the ability to adapt and grow efficiently. In this context, I am going with STRONG BUY as this might be a good growth stock.

data.title: Mach Natural Resources Increases Its Total Production By Nearly 90% Through Acquisitions
data.description:
Mach Natural Resources (MNR) announced a couple major acquisitions with a combined deal price of nearly $1.3 billion. The consideration for the acquisitions is roughly 41% cash and 59% common units.

These deals will increase Mach's total production by close to 90%, while its oil production may increase by approximately 55%. The acquired assets have low base decline rates and limited recent development activity, although there is the potential for some future Mancos Shale development in the San Juan Basin.

Since the acquisitions are partly paid for in cash (bringing Mach's pro forma net debt to nearly $1 billion), it does boost Mach's free cash flow per unit and should support a higher near-term distribution (depending on how much Mach puts towards debt reduction) compared to without the acquisitions.

I now estimate Mach's value at $15.75 to $17.75 per unit, based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. This is a $0.25 per unit increase from before. Mach's units should be a bit more attractive due to the potential for a higher distribution (compared to without the acquisition), although the acquisitions themselves appear to be at a fair price.

Combined Deals

The combined deal price is $1.287 billion, currently consisting of $525 million in cash and 52.35 million common units. Mach's outstanding unit count will increase to approximately 170.7 million common units.

The acquired assets had approximately 71,000 BOEPD in Q1 2025 production, with around 15% of that production being oil, 5% NGLs, and 80% natural gas.

pro forma for the acquisitions, Mach will have approximately 152,000 BOEPD in production, with around 19% of that production being oil, 15% being NGLs and 66% being natural gas.

The acquisitions have a low base decline rate, so this lowers Mach's corporate base decline rate from 20% to 15%.

Mach hasn't provided information about EBITDA and production costs for the acquired assets. I believe the acquisitions are higher cost though (being mature low-decline assets) and estimate the combined lease operating expense and gathering and processing costs for the acquired production at around $14 per BOE. This is a fair bit higher than the roughly $9.50 per BOE combined total (LOE and G&P) for Mach's existing assets, despite the acquired production being less oily.

IKAV San Juan Assets

Mach is paying $787 million (including $325 million in cash) for IKAV Energy's San Juan Basin assets.

The IKAV San Juan assets are mostly natural gas (6% NGLs and 94% natural gas), with total production around 60,000 BOEPD. The PDP base decline rate is low at 10%.

The San Juan Basin has seen renewed activity recently, with six rigs currently active (the most since 2015). The Mancos Shale is a favorite target for new natural gas development there, and Mach indicated that it may operate a rig there starting in spring 2026.

As a comparison, TXO Partners' San Juan Basin assets (which are 17% NGLs and 83% natural gas) have production, transportation, and other costs (excluding taxes) adding up to approximately $13 per BOE.

Sabinal Acquisition

The Sabinal acquisition involves 130,000 net acres. Sabinal's assets are located in the Central Basin Platform as well as the Eastern, Northern, and Northwest Shelves of the Permian Basin.

The Sabinal assets are mostly oil (98% oil) with a low base decline rate (8% PDP base decline). Those assets had Q1 2025 production that may have been around 10,600 BOEPD (rounded up to 11 MBOEPD in Mach's presentation).

The high oil cut and low base decline rate make it likely that the Sabinal assets have relatively high lease operating expenses (probably at least in the $20s per BOE).

There was a June 2022 report that Sabinal was looking to fetch over $1 billion in a sale (and potentially looking for $1.5 billion), and that it could generate over $400 million in forward-year EBITDA. That was with forward-year WTI oil over $100, though.

A rough estimate suggests that the Sabinal assets could generate around $125 million in forward-year EBITDA at the current strip (approximately $64 WTI oil).

Effect On Mach's Results

Mach's total production is expected to increase by close to 90% as a result of the acquisitions, while its oil production may increase by approximately 55%.

Mach is paying for the acquisitions with a combination of cash and common units. Mach's common unit count is currently expected to increase by approximately 44% from the deals, while its net debt may more than double. Mach had $452 million in net debt at the end of Q1 2025 and is paying $525 million in cash for the deals.

At $63 WTI oil and $4.25 NYMEX natural gas (2026 strip), the acquisitions may boost Mach's EBITDA by a bit over 50%, while its total free cash flow may increase by over 60%. This assumes that Mach runs one Mancos Shale rig starting in Spring 2026.

A rough estimate suggests that Mach's free cash flow per unit (at those commodity prices) may increase by around 13% to 14% from the acquisitions.

Notes On Valuation

Mach mentioned its recent acquisitions were at a discount to PDP PV-10, but did not specify how much of a discount. Thus I assume that it was only a modest discount, which would make the acquisitions roughly fairly priced to me.

I am slightly increasing my estimate of Mach's value by $0.25 per unit. This brings Mach's estimated value to $15.75 to $17.75 per unit at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I believe the acquisitions were fairly priced, while Mach's higher estimated near-term free cash flow per unit makes its units a bit more attractive due to the potential effect on distributions. Due to weaker commodity prices, Mach's upcoming distributions are likely to be lower than Q1 2025's $0.79 per unit distribution. I can see Mach generating a bit over $0.65 per unit in quarterly distributable cash flow after the deals close, though.

Mach's ability to replace its reserves (for the acquired assets) will depend on the success of its Mancos Shale development. Mach estimated that it is getting around 100 gross Mancos Shale locations in the deal.

Conclusion

Mach made a couple major transactions that add nearly 90% to its total production. I believe Mach's free cash flow can improve by over 60% too. Mach paid for the acquisitions with a combination of shares and units, so its free cash flow per unit may increase by a low-teens percentage compared to without the acquisitions, while its net debt has more than doubled.

Mach's leverage and debt situation still appear to be reasonable, although Mach may choose to put some of its free cash flow towards debt reduction going forward. That would reduce its quarterly distribution below the roughly $0.65 per unit in free cash flow that I'd expect at current strip prices.

data.title: Opendoor Technologies: Time To Sell The Rally
data.description:
Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) stock has certainly been on an impressive run recently, with the stock quadrupling over the past month:

This is quite the move coming from a company that many people had left for dead not long ago.

There were concerns that the company might be delisted due to its share price falling well below a dollar. Opendoor had already sought authorization for a reverse stock split. And the company's earnings results had been in a clear downtrend as well.

With this huge rebound in OPEN stock, however, investors are reconsidering their views of this real estate company. So, is there any real merit to this recent rebound or should traders be looking to fade the move? I'm in the bearish camp. There's not much here to explain the recent run. And for investors that currently own OPEN stock, this is a great time to consider lightening up on that position.

The Core Business Model Remains Unproven

Opendoor is one of the pioneers of the so-called iBuying model. iBuyers use technology to assess home values. In turn, they use these calculations to make all-cash bids on undervalued homes that can lead to easy and speedy transactions for the selling owners. Opendoor essentially operates as a home flipping company. It has attempted to scale up, buying thousands of homes per quarter and then attempting to resell them at a higher price to secure a reasonable profit on deployed capital.

The company has proprietary algorithms and software which aim to help it find values in the housing market. On paper, this sounds like a clever and perfectly reasonable business plan.

In practice, however, there's little evidence of it working. Opendoor Technologies has consistently lost money since it became a public company:

In fact, the company has lost at least $300 million every year since 2021. Opendoor often struggles to even produce positive adjusted EBITDA. And, in a home owning business, interest and deprecation are both major expenses. If an iBuying company can't generate solid EBITDA margins, it is unlikely to be able to produce favorable returns for its shareholders over time either.

It's not just Opendoor that has been struggling. Other iBuying players have not met expectations. Take rival Offerpad (OPAD) for example:

Offerpad's shares have performed even worse than Opendoor's on a percentage basis.

Meanwhile, larger real estate companies Zillow (Z) and Redfin shut down their iBuying operations in 2021 and 2022, respectively. It may have seemed possible that having well-known firms like Zillow exit the space would give more opportunity for Opendoor to seize the market. But, Opendoor has not capitalized on the opportunity.

Is iBuying A Compelling Business Model?

That may be, in large part, because there simply isn't that profitable of a business to be had here.

Put another way, I see Opendoor having a structural problem in addition to the current cyclical headwinds.

The U.S. housing market is highly competitive. There's no shortage of potential home buyers ranging from individuals and family-run real estate operations up through REITs, private equity, and other larger institutional players. There are plenty of informed buyers around the country. And if I had to bet, on average, I'd guess that savvy local operators who have been involved in a specific market for decades are going to have an informational edge over a national iBuying company that is relying mostly on computer data and predictive algorithms to try to sleuth out bargains. Real estate is all about location, and I'm not convinced AI is at the point where it can beat boots on the ground expertise yet.

Additionally, there are numerous hidden costs in the housing industry as anyone who has tried to renovate a home can probably tell you.

It's great to have a financial model which concludes that a company can make 10% or 15% gross margins on average in its home flipping operations. But, on a large enough pool of homes, you will inevitably run into issues with titles, structural defects, tax issues, changes in the local market, and other such headaches after you buy a property. With house flipping you aren't likely to get too much additional upside compared to your model. The goal is to make your planned profit and sell quickly to recycle the cash into the next deal. Upside is generally capped.

But on the left tail, it's possible to end up with situations where you fall well short of the intended profit and in some cases lose money outright. It's not just the possibility of taking a loss, either, but also of tying up capital in properties that fail to sell as quickly as expected.

Weakening Housing Market Impacts Results

That appears to be occurring now, in fact, as the U.S. housing market has softened. Last quarter, Opendoor reported that its inventory balance had risen to $2.4 billion, representing 7,080 homes, with inventory up 26% versus Q1 of 2024. Its purchases of new homes, however, rose at a much slower pace, indicating that it was ending up holding more inventory.

The most likely cause of having more inventory on hand would be that the company couldn't find buyers for its houses as quickly as expected. We can see that in the financial statements as well. Opendoor disclosed that it had 27% of its homes “on the market” for greater than 120 days at the end of Q1 '25. This was up sharply from the 15% of its homes that were on the market for 120+ days at the end of Q1 '24. This hurts Opendoor in two ways. First, it will likely have to cut prices to unload some of that older inventory. Second, homes that are sitting on the market are tying up the company's invested capital without generating an immediate return.

Seemingly as a result, Opendoor slowed down its buying operations. It ended Q1 with 1,051 homes under contract for purchase. That was down a startling 60% year-over-year and down 38% sequentially from Q4 of 2024. Home flipping is supposed to be a volume business, you make fairly low margins on each house but can profit by moving lots of units. But, as Opendoor slows down its transaction velocity, the profit potential seemingly also diminishes, and its overhead fixed costs will likely consume a larger portion of its overall gross profit.

One of my biggest concerns with Opendoor's business model is that the company was unable to consistently make money even during the huge housing boom we saw over the past few years. To be fair, its rivals like Redfin and Zillow struggled as well, so this is hardly specific to Opendoor. But if the iBuying model wasn't particularly fruitful during the pandemic when home prices were skyrocketing, why would we expect it to fare better in a flat housing market, let alone a potential housing correction?

I'll grant you that iBuyers are likely to see improved algorithms and data processing as AI gets more powerful, and these companies also have a longer track record of past transactions to analyze. Still, if these businesses lost money in a year like 2021 where housing was sizzling, I'm skeptical that they will be strongly profitable anytime soon unless we get an absolutely perfect macroeconomic backdrop.

Admittedly, Opendoor is trying to adjust its business model to improve on its past results. The company's new partnerships with local agents, for example, could improve conversion rates and make the business model a bit less asset-heavy. It's a worthwhile effort on their part. The company is also making adjustments to marketing strategies while slowing down its purchases of new homes. Given the company's past operating losses, this sort of tinkering is worthwhile to see if it can improve results. That said, I'm simply unconvinced the core business model is a particularly strong one even if these adjustments do help at the margins.

Opendoor's Forward Prospects

The most tangible positive I see now is that thanks to the recent run-up in the stock price, the company should have more time to try to figure something out. Previously, based on its cash burn, it seemed like Opendoor could run out of cash as early as 2026. I would guess that now the company will be able to issue enough stock at these higher prices to alleviate those potential funding concerns.

Opendoor will also have more time to wait and see if mortgage rates go back down. That would be the proverbial silver bullet here; if rates drop, that could get the housing market going again and allow Opendoor to clear out its inventory at favorable prices.

That's a reasonable possibility. However, I think there are plenty of other housing stocks that you could own that present a better risk-to-reward ratio if you are primarily wanting to bet on lower interest rates. I'd name title insurers, homebuilders, and building supply companies as three such examples. By contrast, for Opendoor to justify its recent rally, let alone trade higher, you would likely need a major drop in mortgage rates as your base case. On the other hand, if 30-year fixed mortgages stay at 7% on average (let alone go higher), I expect Opendoor to keep running large operating losses and, as a result, for the stock to drop back to below a buck.

At the end of the day, Opendoor has not proven that its business model can be consistently profitable. And other iBuyer peers have also struggled. Publicly-traded home flipping may not be a particularly compelling niche. Until proven otherwise, I remain skeptical.

Bulls may see OPEN stock as the victim of a rough macroeconomic climate. If interest rates drop and the housing market surges again, Opendoor could make a comeback. It's possible. But it seems like a fairly low probability event, and even if housing does boom again, there are other sector bets that should offer better risk-adjusted returns. Meanwhile, Opendoor faces structural challenges, and no amount of positive social media posts or meme stock energy is bound to change that reality.

I have never traded OPEN stock personally. If I had a position, however, I'd be looking to sell into this rally as soon as possible. Despite the large rebound in the share price, Opendoor's fundamental outlook is still quite unappealing.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Unilever: Keep Calm And Carry On
data.description:
My last coverage of Unilever (NYSE:UL, OTCPK:UNLYF) dates back to September 2023. The thesis was that price increases were hurting competitiveness, implying annual free cash flow would remain constant which in turn would put a lid on shareholder returns.

Fast forward nearly two years, and it’s only fair to say the company proved me wrong when it comes to free cash flow. Free cash flow increased substantially as did the stock price. Nevertheless the overall picture is more balanced as dividends and buybacks remained constant, while net debt increased to fund these.

Making up the balance, Unilever is working on its appeal, though not worth buying yet.

Performance

The investor update for 1Q25 was quite brief. The takeaway was that underlying sales growth was maintained, mainly driven by developed markets, see figure 1.

The most prominent feature is that North America accounted for the bulk of sales growth. This signals resilience in this market but in all likelihood Unilever will not be able to fully capitalize on this as the dollar recently depreciated against both the Euro and British Pound.

Examining the numbers further it’s noteworthy volume was the main driver for sales growth in the developed markets. The downside is that Unilever did not match inflation with the price growth.

For emerging markets, where the majority of turnover is generated, volume growth has been disappointing and price increases needed to do the heavy lifting.

Apart from North America, the company is performing a balancing act where volume versus price increases are weighed. This ties in with management highlighting worsening consumer sentiment and heightened commodity costs as potential headwinds this year. On the 31st of July an update will be given but my expectation is that headwinds will prevail.

Growing cash flow

While quarterly performance may be an interesting pointer for short-term performance, over the longer-term Unilever manages to navigate headwinds quite well. If the performance summary from the FY24 report is taken, see figure 2, underlying sales have grown, as have underlying operating profit and free cash flow.

Considering the cash flow statement, it appears profit trended down since 2022, and the increase in cash flow stems mainly from the line items listed below the operating profit, see figure 3. Given the company is in a transition, of which disposals and acquisitions are part, this needn’t be a problem. Moreover, correcting for the line item ‘Elimination of losses/profits on disposal’, the cash flow from operating activities is rather constant over the presented timeframe instead of growing. This way a different conclusion is drawn than merely taking the cash flow growth presented in figure 2 at face value.

With Unilever the focus on underlying sales growth is always front and center. This metric is the combination of underlying volume and price growth. Figure 4 shows the underlying sales growth plotted against inflation metrics for both the US and EU. It was noted before the company derives the bulk of turnover from emerging markets, but in this case I am using the inflation data from developed markets as the quality and availability of data is superior.

The data undoubtedly shows that Unilever follows the inflation trend when it comes to sales growth. This implies the company has sufficient pricing power to avoid inflation chipping away too much from turnover, but it must be remembered sales growth is a combination of price and volume growth. In other words, if sales growth follows inflation, it means part only part is covered by pricing. Considering this, pricing power may be less than perceived.

Given the high inflation numbers, it is worth noting that the cost of sales came down over the last years, a reduction which is mainly attributable to lower costs for packaging materials, see figure 5. On the other hand, to support sales growth, brand and marketing investments increased by 20% in two years’ time. This implies sales growth does not come cheap and requires continuous investment to maintain strong brands and keep the attention of consumers.

All-in all, the performance is quite balanced in spite of the company being in transition. But, even though the company is able to maintain sales growth aligned with inflation, real strength would be displayed when price growth would offset inflationary effects and volume growth would be added on top of this. Currently this is a bridge too far.

Strategy

To address these issues, the company formed a new strategy titled Growth Action Plan. The document makes for a interesting read, but given current knowledge, the presentation of then CFO Fernando Fernandez was more interesting. The CFO turned CEO made an honest assessment of the state of affairs, see figure 6.

To buck these trends the most notable action is the separation of the Ice Cream business. This will support performance and polish up the numbers the year after, but won’t have a recurring effect.

The aforementioned effect of price growth lagging inflation, is addressed implicitly in the 2030 targets. Figure 7 shows underlying sales growth of mid-single digits is targeted, of which at least 2% must be volume growth. Working under the assumption that central banks will be able to maintain inflation in a range of 2-3%, which is to be offset by price increases, and adding the 2% volume growth adds up to 5% growth. In other words, it means that management aims for real volume growth, expanding the business.

The other items seem less ambitious as the results for FY24 plotted against this plan show that the targets have already been achieved, see figure 8.

As the goals in the strategy appear to have been met, the question is how much gain is still to be made for a matured company like Unilever. Based on the data available, management may have its hands full to maintain performance as is.

Shareholder returns

Shareholder returns for Unilever have been a point of debate for years. Last year however the stock price broke out on the back of improved performance, only to gradually subside again, see figure 9.

Year-to-date the stock price recovered again, where performance for US-based investors was propped up by depreciation of the dollar. For European investors the performance has been about 10% less.

The starting point of the graph coincides with the date of my previous coverage. At the time I gave a sell rating, which may seem too cautious given the graph below, but it must be said that total return since was 28% versus a 45% change for the S&P.

Dwelling too long on stock price appreciation may not interest most readers as Unilever is often seen as a stable dividend stock for ones retirement where price fluctuations do not matter as long as the cash keeps coming every quarter. Though that may be too simplistic as the stock price is a measure of performance which also accounts for the ability to deliver consistent dividend increases.

On the back of improved cash flow, management decided to increase the dividend per share. However, while DPS rose, total returns to shareholders remained level over the last three years, see figure 10.

The upside of share buybacks, which the company traditionally did not do, is that these offset the growing dividend implying total cash outflows remain constant. This creates stability which many investors look for in volatile markets.

While buybacks and growing dividends are definitely a good thing, one could also argue inflation is eroding total shareholder return as the sum of both remains constant. On top of this the following statement was made in the most recent annual report:

Closing net debt was €24.5 billion compared to €23.7 billion as at 31 December 2023. The increase was due to capital returns of €4.3 billion in dividends and €1.5 billion in share buybacks to PLC shareholders, and other adverse movements that were partially offset by the free cash flow delivery of €6.9 billion.

In other words, last years’ improved free cash flow was not sufficient to cover (constant) shareholder returns which is not instantly clear by merely plotting the data as done in figure 10.

Risks

Keeping performance and returns constant may be challenging already. Whereas Unilever used volume growth to make-up where pricing can’t do the heavy lifting, in China it’s the other way around. The focus on volume, essentially flooding markets, mean prices need to drop to maintain revenue. This pattern is visible in multiple industries and has been termed involution;

Natixis’ study of 2,500 listed Chinese companies reinforce how volume is growing while value is being hurt by deflationary pressure. (…)

U.S. coffee giant Starbucks has struggled in China with falling sales as it maintains prices of around 30 yuan per cup ($4.20) — while a host of rivals from Luckin Coffee to boutiques sell lattes for as low as 9.9 yuan.

This example says it all, there is a point where strong branding is not able to make-up for low prices. Unilever will be subject to the same dynamics meaning performance in Asia Pacific may remain poor.

Looking at developed markets, North America performed well, but on the back of a depreciating dollar the income derived from this geography will reduce measured in British Pounds. The depreciation went fast meaning Unilever may not be able to offset this by price increases without hurting volume in the short term. This would complicate reaching the set targets in the short term.

Conclusion

At first glance, Unilever has been upping performance over the last three years. However, correcting for the effect of disposals, cash flow is rather constant over the presented time-frame.

This should not be surprising as sales growth followed inflation. Real strength occurs when price growth offsets inflationary effects and volume growth would be added on top of this.

The lack of strength resulted in total shareholders returns, dividend and buybacks, remaining constant over the last three years.

The company is executing its strategy to improve performance, but the stated goals were achieved last year already. Continued sales growth is the most challenging goal, but management may have its hands full to maintain performance as is. The upside however is that management acknowledges lagging performance and is on a mission to improve. Let’s wait and see.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
